cards.text: The {{c1::break}} statement causes a switch or loop to terminate immediately, and execution continues with the next statement after the switch or loop.

cards.front: What does break do in a loop?
cards.back: It immediately exits the loop, and execution continues with the first statement after the loop.

cards.question: What does this code print?
cards.explanation: When i equals 5, break exits the loop immediately. Numbers 0-4 are printed, then the loop terminates.

cards.text: The {{c1::continue}} statement causes the current iteration of a loop to end, and execution jumps to the next iteration.

cards.front: What is the difference between break and continue in a loop?
cards.back: Break exits the loop entirely. Continue skips the rest of the current iteration and moves to the next iteration.

cards.question: What does this code print?
cards.explanation: When i is even, continue skips the cout and jumps to the next iteration. Only odd numbers are printed.

cards.front: In nested loops, what does break affect?
cards.back: Only the innermost loop containing the break. It does not exit outer loops.

cards.question: What does this code print?
cards.code:
for (int i = 0; i < 3; ++i)
{
    for (int j = 0; j < 3; ++j)
    {
        if (j == 1)
            break;
        std::cout << i << j << ' ';
    }
}
cards.explanation: Break only exits the inner loop when j==1. The outer loop continues normally. Each outer iteration prints the ij pair where j=0.

cards.front: In a for loop, what happens after continue executes?
cards.back: Execution jumps to the end-expression (e.g., ++i), then the condition is checked before the next iteration.

cards.front: What is the key difference in how continue works between for loops and while loops?
cards.back: In for loops, continue jumps to the end-expression. In while loops, it jumps directly to the condition check (potentially skipping update code).

cards.concept: When to use break in loops
cards.explanation:
Use break when:
  • You find what you're searching for and can exit early
  • An error condition occurs that makes continuing pointless
  • You want to exit an intentional infinite loop (while(true))
  • You need early termination based on a complex condition
Don't overuse - if the loop condition can express the exit naturally, use that instead.

cards.concept: When to use continue in loops
cards.explanation:
Use continue when:
  • You want to skip certain iterations based on a condition
  • It makes the code more readable than nested ifs
  • You're filtering out unwanted values
Be careful with while loops - continue skips the update statement, which can cause infinite loops if you're not careful.
examples:
// Dangerous in while loop
while (i < 10) {
    if (skip) continue; // skips ++i!
    ++i;
}

cards.front: Can you use break and continue outside of loops or switch statements?
cards.back: No, break and continue can only be used inside loops (break also works in switch). Using them elsewhere is a compile error.
